coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of high quantum yield rhenium-doped carbonized polymer dots for dual sensing of Fe3+ and Mo6+ and anti-counterfeit ink applications
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yushu
Kong, Xin
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Liu, Liang
Gu, Ying
Yi, Lunzhao
coredata.dc:description: The bottom-up approach serves as an efficacious and noteworthy method for the synthesis of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). In the present investigation, rhenium-doped CPDs (Re-CPDs) were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal technique employing citric acid, urea, and NH4ReO4. Subsequent to a comprehensive series of characterizations, Re-CPDs demonstrated an average particle size of 2.67 nm, excitation/emission maxima of 377/461 nm, and an elevated quantum yield of 45.36% at 377 nm excitation. Through the selectivity analysis involving various metal ions, Re-CPDs displayed sensitivity towards Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions, with limits of detection (LODs) of 0.02 μM and 0.48 μM, respectively. Furthermore, Re-CPDs exhibited multi-chromatic fluorescence (450–550 nm) under excitation wavelengths (375–430 nm). As a result, by amalgamating Re-CPDs with sucrose, detection patterns capable of generating multi-chromatic fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 375, 395, and 430 nm, respectively, were successfully devised. In summary, Re-CPDs hold considerable potential as a material for the detection of Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions, as well as for anti-counterfeiting ink applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhenium
Carbonized polymer dots
Fe3+ ions sensor
Mo6+ ions sensor
Anti-counterfeit ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ ions in real samples
Real samples detection of Fe3+ and Mo6+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52064032 , 52174345 ), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202202AG050004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Re-CPDs (inset: the particle size distribution). (b) HRTEM image of the Re-CPDs. (c) TEM image of CPDs (inset: the particle size distribution). (d) HRTEM image of the CPDs.

(a) XRD patterns of Re-CPDs and CPDs. (b) Raman patterns of the Re-CPDs and CPDs. (c) FTIR image of Re-CPDs. (d) XPS survey of Re-CPDs. (e) C1s, (f) N1s, (g) O1s, and (h) Re 4f XPS spectra. (i) Zeta potential of the Re-CPDs and CPDs.

(a) Research on multiple ions selection by Re-CPDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra corresponding to the addition of different contents of Fe3+ to Re-CPDs (from 0 to 40 μM). (c) Relationship between Fe3+ concentration in range of 0–1.0 μM and fluorescence intensity. (d) Compared fluorescence lifetime with Re-CPDs + Fe3+ and Re-CPDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra corresponding to the addition of different contents of Mo6+ to Re-CPDs (from 0 to 9 μM). (b) Relationship between Mo6+ concentration in range of 0–9 μM and fluorescence intensity. (c) Compared fluorescence lifetime with Re-CPDs + Mo6+ and Re-CPDs.
Fluorescence graphs of Re-CPDs + Sucrose anti-counterfeiting inks under (a) 377 nm, (b) 395 nm and (c) 430 nm excitation, respectively.
Optical analysis to detect Fe3+ ions.
Optical analysis to detect Mo6+ ions.
Recovery of Fe3+ added to milk measured by Re-CPDs (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N/A: Not applicable; a: mean value of three determinations; b: standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of t Mo6+ added to milk examined by Re-CPDs (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N/A: Not applicable; a: mean value of three determinations; b: standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of high quantum yield rhenium-doped carbonized polymer dots for dual sensing of Fe3+ and Mo6+ and anti-counterfeit ink applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Material Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The bottom-up approach serves as an efficacious and noteworthy method for the synthesis of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). In the present investigation, rhenium-doped CPDs (Re-CPDs) were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal technique employing citric acid, urea, and NH4ReO4. Subsequent to a comprehensive series of characterizations, Re-CPDs demonstrated an average particle size of 2.67 nm, excitation/emission maxima of 377/461 nm, and an elevated quantum yield of 45.36% at 377 nm excitation. Through the selectivity analysis involving various metal ions, Re-CPDs displayed sensitivity towards Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions, with limits of detection (LODs) of 0.02 μM and 0.48 μM, respectively. Furthermore, Re-CPDs exhibited multi-chromatic fluorescence (450–550 nm) under excitation wavelengths (375–430 nm). As a result, by amalgamating Re-CPDs with sucrose, detection patterns capable of generating multi-chromatic fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 375, 395, and 430 nm, respectively, were successfully devised. In summary, Re-CPDs hold considerable potential as a material for the detection of Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions, as well as for anti-counterfeiting ink applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) possess high fluorescence intensity, good stability, and tunable emission spectra, rendering them extensively utilized in photocatalysis, bioimaging, and sensors, and an integral part of new light-emitting nanomaterials [1]. Hybrid atom doping has emerged as a crucial strategy for augmenting the energy band gap and electronic structure of CPDs. Typically, heteroatom-doped CPDs are synthesized via hydrothermal [2], microwave [3], and solvothermal methods [4].

Previous studies have demonstrated that transition metal ions furnish more valence electrons than non-metal ions and more efficiently modify the sp2 domains of CPDs during the doping process, resulting in CPDs with outstanding optical properties and functionalization [5]. For instance, Zhang et al. [6] doped cerium into carbon nanodots (Ce-CNDs) to produce an antibacterial and anti-inflammatory therapeutic nanoplatform to accelerate healing, and Rong et al. [7] selected manganese doped into carbon dots (Mn-CDs) to design a scaled biosensor for DPA detection. Rhenium (Re) possesses a small ionic radius, an abundance of valence electrons, strong polarizability, and exhibits immense potential in functionalizing CPDs. Currently, the doping of rhenium has garnered new research attention in various fields such as catalysis [8], bioimaging [9], and drug delivery [10].

The paramount and focused utilization of metal-doped carbon dots lies in the detection of trace metal ions, including Fe3+ and Mo6+, which are essential trace elements for human physiology and implicated in diverse pathologies such as anemia, nephrolithiasis, and diabetes [11–13]. Furthermore, it is critical to monitor the levels of Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions in water for scientific assessment and prediction of water environment management, pollution source control, and ecological development. Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), mass spectrometry, and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) are widely utilized for detecting ions in water [14,15], but their complex application, long test cycles, high instrument costs, and inability to provide data easily and quickly limit their practical application [16]. Consequently, simple operation, fast testing, inexpensive and sensitive detection methods are urgently needed today. Metal ions doped CPDs have been applied for metal ion detection, such as Gao et al. [17], who employed Fe doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) for Fe3+ ion detection, and Zhang et al. [18], who used lanthanum doped carbon dots in (La-CQDs) for detecting Hg2+ ions.

Metal-doped carbon dots represent a promising avenue for developing anti-counterfeiting ink, which can be leveraged to thwart the production of counterfeit banknotes by unscrupulous actors [19], identify fake products [20], and prevent the use of fraudulent documents in the commission of crimes [21]. For instance, Bhati et al. [22] synthesized safe and stable fluorescent inks by doping magnesium in carbon dots (Mg-CDs), and Kumari et al. [23] created multicolor fluorescent inks by doping manganese in carbon dots (Mn-CDs). Hence, metal ion doped CPDs have promising applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting inks. To the best of our knowledge, no research has been documented on the utilization of Re-CPDs in ion detection sensors and fluorescent encryption.

In this study, Re-CPDs were prepared via hydrothermal method using citric acid, urea and ammonium perrhenate. Excitation dependence was observed at 461 nm (relative QY = 45.36%). The pH and photobleaching experiments demonstrated that Re-CPDs are stable and tolerant to strong alkaline environments. Re-CPDs were capable of sensing Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.02 μM and 0.48 μM, respectively. Moreover, Re-CPDs were combined with sucrose to synthesize multi-color anti-counterfeit inks with adjustable fluidity (Scheme 1 ).

The experimental solvent was prepared using deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm. All mental salinization reagents, such as FeCl3·6H2O (98%), CuCl2·2H2O (99%), NaCl (99%), AlCl3·6H2O (99%), KNO3 (99%), LiCl (99%), NiCl2·6H2O (99.9%), H3BO3 (99.5%), C4H6MgO4 (99%), C4H6MnO4 (99%), Na2MoO4 (98%), C4H6Co·4H2O (99%), and C4H6O4Zn·2H2O (98%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Coꓻ Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (99.5%), urea (99%) and NH4ReO4 (99%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Coꓻ Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In addition, sucrose (99%) and L-Ascorbic acid (99%) were also purchased from Shanghai Macklin Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of the substances used in our experiment were of analytical grade and were not purified.

Re-CPDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process. Specifically, 90 ml of deionized water was mixed with 1.34 g NH4ReO4, 1.20 g urea, and 4.80 g citric acid, and the mixture was stirred using a magnetic mixer at room temperature for 30 min. The homogenous mixture was then placed into a TGYF-A-micro reactor lined with Teflon and heated to 160 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, reddish-brown suspension was filtered through a 0.1 μM microporous membrane to remove larger impurities. To obtain a purer Re-CPDs sample, the filtered Re-CPDs solution was put into 500 Da dialysis bags and continuously dialyzed for 24 h. The resulting Re-CPDs solution was freeze-dried for 36 h and transformed into a powder for further quantitative investigation. CPDs were synthesized using the same procedures as Re-CPDs, but without the addition of NH4ReO4.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Re-CPDs were determined and assessed using the UV-1700PA UV absorption spectrometer (Shanghai Macylab Instrument Co. Ltd.). All fluorescence spectra were obtained using the fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 apparatus. To analyze the surface components of Re-CPDs, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by PHI5000 VersaprobeIII, with a working voltage of 15 eV and an X-ray source was Al Kα. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was employed to probe the chemical bonding information of powdered Re-doped CPDs. Raman spectra were collected using Thermo Scientific DXR 2xi Micro-Raman measurements with a 532 nm laser. The morphology of the powder Re-CPDs samples was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime samples were detected by FLS1000, with a concentration of 1.0 mg/ml.

In a typical experiment, 0.1 g Re-CPDs was added into 100 ml of deionized water, and its fluorescence intensity was recorded as F0. Then, different Fe3+ concentrations were added into the Re-CPDs (1 mg/ml) solution and shaken until homogeneous. The effect of Fe3+ was removed by adding L-Ascorbic acid to recover the Re-CPDs. The recovery of Re-CPDs was further determined by five sets of replicate tests. At room temperature Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+, Al3+, K+, Li+, Ni2+, B3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Mo6+, Co2+, and Zn2+ were added to the solution of Re-CPDs at a concentration of 12 μM for selectivity testing.

Relative fluorescence quantum yield of Re-CPDs and CPDs was determined by the following equation [24]:
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
(
G r a d
S
G r a d
R
)
(
η S 2
η R 2
)

Here, Φ and Grad denote the quantum yield and integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance, respectively. η represents the refractive index of the solvent in the atmosphere. Subscripts “R” and “S” indicate reference standard and samples, respectively. Flash quantum yield of deionized water measured at an excitation wavelength of 377 nm was chosen as the reference standard.
η S
/
η R
is approximately equal to 1.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To simulate real water samples, milk from our laboratory in Yunnan, China was utilized by adding varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions to evaluate the effectiveness of Re-CPDs. The detection of Fe3+ in water samples was based on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0–F)/F0 measured using the F-7000 spectrophotometer. A mixture of Re-CPDs and milk with a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL was prepared for subsequent experiments. Finally, the Fe3+ ion content in milk was determined using the deionized water ion detection method mentioned above.

The structure, morphology, and dimensions of Re-CPDs and CPDs were characterized and investigated using TEM. The Re-CPDs in Fig. 1 a are roughly spherical with a particle size distribution ranging from 1.4 to 3.3 nm, and an average diameter of 2.61 nm (see Fig. 1a inset). The high-resolution TEM image clearly shows the lattice spacing of Re-CPDs to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphene (Fig. 1b). The CPDs nanoparticles in Fig. 1c are distributed in the size range of 1.8–3.4 nm, with an average diameter of 2.76 nm (see Fig. 1c inset). The HRTEM image displays round structures with a weakly crystalline lattice spacing of 0.21 nm as shown in (Fig. 1d).

The XRD patterns of CPDs and Re-CPDs exhibit a typical amorphous carbon diffraction peak at 21.7°, demonstrating the presence of the (100) face of graphite in both Fig. 2 a. The Raman spectra of CPDs and Re-CPDs show two distinct peaks at 1354 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 [25], which correspond to the D band and G band, respectively (Fig. 2b). In this experiment, the ID/IG of Re-CPDs is 0.829 and the ID/IG of CPDs is 0.711, indicating that the oxygen-containing defect on the surface of the CPDs is amplified by the doping of Re element.

The functional groups and surface chemical compositions of CPDs (Fig. S2) and Re-CPDs (Fig. 2c) were determined by FT-IR spectroscopy. The board absorption peaks of CPDs and Re-CPDs at 3205 and 2935 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of N–H and C–H/O–H, respectively. Additionally, Re-CPDs absorption peaks at 1697 cm−1 and 1537 cm−1 relate to the stretching vibrations CO and CC, respectively. However, the absorption peak of CPDs at 1693 cm−1 belongs to CO/CC stretching vibration. The absorption peaks of CPDs and Re-CPDs at 1405 cm−1 and 1177 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–N and C–O, respectively. Importantly, the Re–O absorption peak formed by Re-CPDs in 764 cm−1 further proves that Re metal ions have been successfully doped in CPDs [26].

The elemental composition and valence states of CPDs and Re-CPDs were analyzed using XPS. The total XPS spectral of Re-CPDs indicate 283.9 eV, 399.1 eV, and 530.2 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s (Fig. 2d), as well as Re4f orbitals and atomic percentages of C, N, O, and Re at 70.0%, 0.2%, 29.7%, and 0.1%, respectively. High-resolution C 1s spectra at 283.4, 283.9, and 284.5 eV form-fitted peaks which belong to C–O/C–N, CC/C–C, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2e). Formation of fitted peaks at 397.2 and 398.1 eV in high-resolution N1s spectra attributed to Pyridinic-N and Graphite-N, respectively (Fig. 2f) [27]. In high-resolution O1s spectra, the fitting peaks for Re–O, CO, and C–OH/O–C–O are produced at 529.5, 530.1, and 530.8 eV (Fig. 2g). The high-resolution Re4f spectrum at 39.5, 41.6, and 42.6 eV were fitted peaks corresponding to Re 4f7/2, Re 4f5/2, and Re–O, respectively (Fig. 2h).

In contrast, the XPS spectrum of CPDs demonstrated that CPDs consist of 53.2%, 2.6% and 44.2% atomic percentages of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, respectively (Fig. S2). The C1s spectra exhibited separated fitted peaks of CC/C–C (283.4 eV), C–O/C–N (285.2 eV), and CO (287.2 eV). The N 1s spectra yielded fitted peaks of Pyrolitic-N (398.1 eV), Pyridinic-N (398.6 eV), and Graphite-N (399.8 eV). The O1s spectrum produced fitted peaks for CO (530.4 eV) and C–OH/O–C–O (531.8 eV) (Fig. S2).

According to the FT-IR and XPS analyses of CPDs and Re-CPDs, Re-CPDs not only contain similar nitrogen group, carboxyl group and graphitic carbon structure as CPDs, but also successfully incorporate Re element into CPDs. To understand the effect of Re doping on the surface charge of CPDs, experiments were conducted on zeta potential (ξ) [28]. The zeta potential of CPDs was −21.20 mV, while that of Re-CPDs is −4.28 mV (Fig. 2i), attributable to the successful doping of Re elements that reduced the negative charge on the surface of CPDs [29].

The optical properties of CPDs and Re-CPDs were investigated using UV–Vis, PL and PLE spectroscopy as detailed in the Supplementary Material. Moreover, the concentration of the Re-CPDs for subsequent experiments was set at 1 mg/ml. The Re-CPDs solution (1 mg/ml) was then subjected to the 365 nm light source for photobleaching experiments, which resulted in a decrease in fluorescence intensity to 93.54% after 120 min of light exposure (Fig. S4).

The aim of this study was to investigate whether Re-CPDs can distinguish specific metal ions via the abundant carboxyl and amino functional groups on their surfaces. The selectivity of Re-CPDs was tested with Al3+, Cu2+, K+, Li+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe3+, B3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Mo6+, and Zn2+ and the fluorescence intensity of Re-CPDs (1 mg/ml) using F0, which represents the fluorescence intensity of Re-CPDs without the addition of any metal ions, which represents the fluorescence intensity obtained by adding the same amount of different metal ions (12 μM) to the Re-CPDs solution under the same conditions (Fig. S5).

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the calculated equation (F– F0)/F0 indicated that the fluorescence intensity of Re-CPDs solution decreased by more than 86% with the addition of Fe3+, and the fluorescence intensity of Re-CPDs was enhanced by 45% with Mo6+ ions. However, the other metal ions did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of Re-CPDs. This result demonstrated that Re-CPDs are selective for Fe3+ and Mo6+.

Different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–40 μM) were added to the Re-CPDs solution, and the PL intensity (461 nm) was evaluated to assess the sensitivity of Re-CPDs to Fe3+ (Fig. S6). With increasing Fe3+ concentrations, the PL intensity of Re-CPDs consistently decreased (Fig. 3b), and a linear relationship (y = 0.6902x+0.0022, R2 = 0.9984) formed between Fe3+ concentration (0.1–1.0 μM) and PL intensity, yielding a 0.02 μM LOD for Re-CPDs. (Fig. 3c).

The quenching of fluorescence caused by the binding of quenching agents to Re-CPDs can usually be distinguished into static quenching and dynamic quenching [36]. In this study, the Stern-Volmer equation was chosen to distinguish between these two types of quenching [37]:
(2)
K Q
=
K
S V
τ 0

KQ is dynamic quenching rate constant (2 × 1010 L/(mol⋅s)), less than which is the dynamic quenching, and vice versa is static quenching. KSV is Stern-Volmer constant (3.68 × 10−3 mol/L), and τ0 is the average fluorescence lifetime.

As shown in Fig. 3d, the average fluorescence lifetime of Re-CPDs decreased from 5.58 ns to 5.27 ns as the Fe3+ content increased, and KQ (7 × 105 L/(mol⋅s)) calculated from the above equation was much less than the dynamic quenching rate constant, therefore, it was a dynamic quenching between Re-CPDs and Fe3+ (Fig. S8).

The quenching mechanism of Re-CPDs in Fe3+ ions was carried out by analyzing both surface morphology and electron transfer of Re-CPDs. The surface of Re-CPDs is rich in a large number of carboxyl and amino functional groups, which combine with Fe3+ to form chelates, changing the division of excitons in Re-CPDs. This results in charge and electron transfer between Re-CPDs and Fe3+, promoting non-radiative recombination of excitons, and eventually result in fluorescence quenching. In previous reports, L-Ascorbic acid (AA) was able to bind to Fe3+ and was able to eliminate Fe3+ from CPDs, allowing CPDs to regain fluorescence. Therefore, we used AA to determine the recycling characteristics of Re-CPDs. AA was added to quenching Re-CPDs solution (AA: Fe3+ = 1:1), and fluorescence intensity recovered to 95.6% after five replicate experiments (Fig. S9). The schematic diagram of this ON-OFF-ON process is summarized in Fig. S10.

Interestingly, the addition of Mo6+ ions significantly increase the PL intensity of Re-CPDs. We inferred that the enrichment of functional groups (amino and carboxyl groups) on the surface of Re-CPDs capture Mo6+ to form stable compounds that change the exciton division in Re-CPDs. The similarity of Re and Mo atomic orbitals leads to compounds with reduced non-radiative recombination of excitons during charge transfer, which eventually leads to an increase in PL intensity of Re-CPDs and reduces the impact of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) (Fig. S13).

Furthermore, the addition of Mo6+ ions to Re-CPDs solution also increased the fluorescence lifetime of Re-CPDs to 5.74 ns (Fig. 4c). Overall, Re-CPDs have the potential to serve as a new sensor with cheap, sensitive and high selectivity for Mo6+ ions.

In addition, Re-CPDs were subjected to titration with HCl and NaOH solutions to change the pH from 2.0 to 13.0, and the obtained PL intensity change signal is shown in (Fig. S14). Although no linear pattern was observed, the PL reached a maximum at pH = 12.0, suggesting the great potential of Re-CPDs for high fluorescence in a strong alkaline environment.
The mechanism of Re-CPDs synthesis is detailed in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real samples detection of Fe3+ and Mo6+

ce:section.ce:para: Re-CPDs were supplemented with sucrose (Re-CPDs + Sucrose) to modulate the fluidity of Re-CPDs, and details of their optical properties are in the Supplementary Material.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a-d), the printed illustrations and graphics employing Re-CPDs + Sucrose ink underwent a color alteration from blue (Ex = 377 nm) to light blue (Ex = 395 nm) and ultimately to green (Ex = 430 nm) when excited by light sources ranging from 375–430 nm. Consequently, we successfully developed the Re-CPDs + Sucrose multi-color anti-counterfeiting ink exhibiting regulated fluidity. As demonstrated in Fig. S17, the integration of sucrose (0.1 g/ml) into Re-CPDs yielded favorable non-adhesive fluidity, leaving no residue on the disposable dropper. However, when the sucrose concentration in Re-CPDs reached 0.9 g/ml, the fluidity of Re-CPDs + Sucrose decreased markedly, resulting in a considerable liquid residue on the disposable dropper. It is evident that incorporating various sucrose concentrations into the Re-CPDs solution can effectively regulate the flow characteristics of Re-CPDs + Sucrose inks, thus tailoring them for more diverse printing environments.

In summary, this paper presents the first successful preparation of Re-CPDs using a hydrothermal method based on citric acid, urea, and NH4ReO4. The obtained Re-CPDs exhibit good water dispersion, an average particle size of 2.67 nm, excitation dependence (abundant surface functional groups), and a high quantum yield of 45.36%. The Re-CPDs are sensitive to Fe3+ ions, exhibiting a linear equation within the range of 0.1–1.0 μM, with an LOD of only 0.02 μM. Additionally, the Re-CPDs are also sensitive to Mo6+ ions, with a linear relationship in the concentration range of 1–9 μM and an LOD of only 0.48 μM, demonstrating that the Re-CPDs are excellent sensors for Fe3+ and Mo6+ ions. Furthermore, ACQ effect of the Re-CPDs was effectively reduced by the addition of sucrose, resulting in an increase in the PL intensity of Re-CPDs + Sucrose (62%) while maintaining excitation dependence, resulting in preparation of Re-CPDs + Sucrose anti-counterfeit inks with tunable fluidity and multi-color fluorescence.

Yushu Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Design and conceived the project, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xin Kong: Conceptualization, Methodology. Rui Bao: Funding acquisition, Designed and conceived the project, Writing - review & editing. Jianhong Yi: Funding acquisition, Investigation. Liang Liu: Validation, Software, Supervision. Ying Gu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lunzhao Yi: Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing. All authors participated in the discussion and commented on the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345), the Yunnan Science and Technology Projects (Grant No. 202202AG050004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal ions-doped carbon dots: synthesis, properties, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, Y. Fu, S. Zhao, J. Xiao, M. Lan, B. Wang, K. Zhang, X. Song, L. Zeng, Metal ions-doped carbon dots: Synthesis, properties, and applications, Chemical Engineering Journal 430 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Xia, S. Zhu, T. Feng, M. Yang, B. Yang, Evolution and Synthesis of Carbon Dots: From Carbon Dots to Carbonized Polymer Dots, Adv Sci (Weinh) 6(23) (2019) 1901316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in chiral carbonized polymer dots: from synthesis and properties to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ru, L. Ai, T.T. Jia, X.J. Liu, S.Y. Lu, Z.Y. Tang, B. Yang, Recent advances in chiral carbonized polymer dots: From synthesis and properties to applications, Nano Today 34 (2020) 100953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation and luminescence in carbonized polymer dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Kang, S. Tao, F. Yang, B. Yang, Aggregation and luminescence in carbonized polymer dots, Aggregate 3(2) (2022) e169.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal ion detection by carbon dots-A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Batool, H.M. Junaid, S. Tabassum, F. Kanwal, K. Abid, Z. Fatima, A.T. Shah, Metal Ion Detection by Carbon Dots-A Review, Crit Rev Anal Chem 52(4) (2022) 756-767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional cerium doped carbon dots nanoplatform and its applications for wound healing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, X. Zhai, T. Ma, Y. Huang, C. Yan, Y. Du, Multifunctional cerium doped carbon dots nanoplatform and its applications for wound healing, Chemical Engineering Journal 423 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescence visual test paper for an anthrax biomarker based on functionalized manganese-doped carbon dots
